title: Latent Planning Meets Autoregressive Generation
subtitle: Training custom transformers to think before they speak
excerpt: What happens when you give a language model the ability to plan in latent space before committing tokens? I've been training custom transformer architectures on Apple MPS to explore exactly this — and the results challenge some assumptions about autoregressive generation.

content.text: Most language models generate text one token at a time, left to right, without any explicit planning mechanism. Each token is conditioned on everything before it, but the model has no way to 'look ahead' or structure its output before committing to the next word.

This is a fundamental limitation. When you write an essay, you don't start with the first word and hope the last paragraph makes sense — you plan. You have a thesis, an outline, a sense of where the argument needs to go. Current autoregressive models lack this entirely.
The Latent Planning Hypothesis

My research explores a simple but powerful idea: what if we give models a dedicated 'planning' step in latent space before they begin generating? Instead of immediately producing tokens, the model first produces a compressed plan — a latent representation of the overall structure — and then conditions its autoregressive generation on that plan.

I've been training custom transformer architectures on Apple Silicon (MPS backend) using PyTorch and HuggingFace. The architecture uses a contrastive loss function to ensure that the latent plan is meaningfully different from a simple summary, and attention map attribution to verify that the model actually uses the plan during generation.
Early Results

The early results are promising. Models with latent planning show improved coherence over long sequences, better adherence to complex instructions, and — most surprisingly — improved factual consistency. The plan seems to act as a soft constraint that keeps the model 'on track' even when the autoregressive process would otherwise drift.

There's still much work to do. The planning mechanism adds computational overhead, and it's not yet clear how to optimally balance plan quality against generation speed. But the direction feels right — giving models the ability to think before they speak is a step toward more reliable, more controllable AI systems.

title: On Invisible Problems
subtitle: Documenting the drift before the stink
excerpt: When I ask myself whether I can still be whatever I want to be, the honest answer isn't really about me. It's about whether there's some invisible problem quietly drifting in the system that makes that statement untrue for someone else.

content.text: Growing up, I was told what most kids hear: you can be whatever you want to be. It was a rich thing to say in 2009, in the middle of a financial crisis that had just proven how fragile our systems actually were. I didn't have the language for it then, but the phrase always felt incomplete — like it came with an asterisk no one wanted to print.
I think about that phrase the same way I think about the River Thames.

By the mid-1800s, London had optimized for growth the way any ambitious system does — fast, optimistic, willing to push its problems downstream. Literally. The river was where the city put what it didn't want to look at. Two people noticed. Edwin Chadwick spent years documenting the coming collapse in reports parliament ignored. John Martin painted apocalyptic scenes of cities overwhelmed by their own waste — not for money, but because he needed people to see it. Both were dismissed as alarmists. Then came the summer of 1858, when the stink from the Thames got so bad that parliament physically couldn't sit in session. The smell forced the redesign the arguments never could.

I love that story. Not because of the collapse — because of Chadwick and Martin. They knew. They said so. They were ignored. And they kept going long enough that when the system finally broke, the blueprint for the fix was already on the table.

That's the part I keep coming back to as an adult. When I ask myself whether I can still be whatever I want to be, the honest answer isn't really about me. It's about whether there's some invisible problem quietly drifting in the system that makes that statement untrue for someone else. For a lot of someones.
That question is how I ended up in tech for good.

I didn't pick systems thinking because it was going to make me money. I picked it because I want to be the kind of person Chadwick and Martin were — someone who documents the drift before the stink, and keeps building even when no one's listening yet. Not because they were better than the parliament that ignored them, but because they cared enough to outlast being dismissed.
If I could hand the next generation one phrase to replace the one I grew up with, it wouldn't be you can be whatever you want to be. It would be a question:
What's something you wish you could do for your friend that you don't think they would notice?
That's where the work is.

title: Family, Friends, and Gemeinschaft
subtitle: A reflection on nostalgia, third places, and societal shifts
excerpt: I wonder now, as an adult, where do I return to in order to celebrate my existence in a place that offers no permanence. Is it a loss of remembrance that I'm grieving?

content.text: When I was a child I remember my birthday party vividly. It was at a McDonald's playground in the early years of 2003. Bush was President, and the net worth to own a burger for a meal didn't cost the stock of a new startup. I wonder now, as an adult, where do I return to in order to celebrate my existence in a place that offers no permanence. Blockbuster offered free rentals for every good grade you earned. Toys R Us gave you discounts on your birthday. Now I'll be lucky if I even wake up having someone text me happy birthday without Facebook reminding them. Is it a loss of remembrance that I'm grieving?

Gemeinschaft / Gesellschaft (Tönnies). Not community vs. society in the colloquial sense. Gemeinschaft is a mode of association based on natural will, kinship, shared locale, and unconscious tradition. Gesellschaft is based on rational will, contract, exchange, and instrumental calculation. I had the will to explore a place that offered no protection. We often feel as if these third places, coined by Oldenburg (1989), truly made us feel as if there was social neutral ground. A toy store on a child's birthday was heaven on earth. It was a place with no hierarchy, a place that was sacred. I don't believe they went away because they became inconvenient. Eventually, as I got older, a DVD was now megabytes in flash memory, practically invisible, with the same richness in playback. Incentives misaligned.

The mid-century compromise (1945–1975) offered the working and middle class a version of access: home ownership, pensions, union protection, public universities, civic organizations, shared physical infrastructure like public pools and libraries. The elite never agreed to share infrastructure permanently. They offered a temporary settlement and then rolled it back.
So I ask myself as an adult: do I remember myself in the moment with my friends, or do I miss the idea of thick places saturated with memory, relationship, habit, and affect?
